sentences.text: Well, it just came, came to, back to me, asking what I remembered about my father.
There was one instant where he was walking through the streets.
And he was grabbed by Germans.
And he was taken to the barber shop to cut his beard off.
And for those people that-- if you know any orthodox people, Hasidic people, it's one of the worst things they can experience to have their beards cut off.
And I remember, when he they was taking him to the barber shop, and the barber started cutting off his beard.
And he finished halfway, one side, and the German left the room.
So my father put a scarf on, as though he had an earache, and he went out in the street.
That's what I remember about my father.
Yes.
The other thing I forgot to mention, at the beginning, where it was still a time before the war, and you asked me, what sect my-- Hasidic sect of my father?
I remember, my father taking me one day-- one year to see the rabbi.
And we went by train to [POLISH].
[POLISH] is just outside Warsaw.
And he bought golden cherries, the white cherries.
And every time I see them now, I have to buy them, whatever the price is.
And I remember my father.
The other thing is, when we moved into our home, the last home we lived in, before the war, was a brick-built.
And the rabbi came for what we call [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the housewarming, to bless our house.
And of course, the whole family moved out.
And it was just the rabbi and his close-- And I remember sitting on his lap.
And he gave me a coin, we call [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], blessed-- a blessed coin.
And as far as my mother was concerned, then, that was the holiest thing she could-- she could have had.
I don't know, maybe because of that, I'm-- I'm here to tell the tale, to tell the little story that-- the few things I remember.
My mother was a very righteous woman.
People tell me she used to fast Mondays and Thursdays.
I don't know what happened.
I don't know what happened.
I must have left behind it with so many other things.
No, that just I can't seem to remember, particularly, on-- on this one.
But this is important, you know, to-- to how she felt about the rabbi.
And I was sat on his lap.
And the joy that she had in that.
Yeah.
Well, he-- it-- it-- my father must have been one of his great supporters.
Because I don't think he did it to everybody.
Obviously, it was a great honor, yes.
And not only that, when this particular rabbi died, he-- I was told-- he left a will to say, first of all, they should wait for my father, for the burial, to come.
And also, there should be a prayer house in our house.
And during the war, there was.
Yeah, I remember one year, it must have been either Yom Kippur-- Rosh Hashanah or Yom Kippur.
In the middle of the service, we heard the commotion, the Germans were knocking on doors.
And some people hid.
Some people stayed in the house.
And some were taken out.
And the fate of those people, who were taken out, well-known now.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes, yes.
There was always strangers used to come to-- to stand by the doorway.
And as you went out, people, we used to invite them-- invite them home.
Yes. you always considered different people.
Ah, I remember things, when my mother used to cook, especially for-- for people, poor people.
And we, as children, used to take canned food down to them.
And we'd be going to one woman, who was living in an attic, and we were taking-- we were walking up on, partly, a ladder to reach her.
And we were taking food.
It was a great joy for us, children.
No.
No.
No.
Seems to be complete blockage of the dates and names in the-- I remember things but never the dates or anything.
But when we get together, surprising enough, when we get together with other boys, we always talking.
Things come to-- to mind, you know, of what happened.
I mean recently, my friend, Monty Tabachnik, reminded me that, when we went back from-- after liberation-- I'm going on with it-- after liberation, at Theresienstadt, him and I went back to Poland.
Somehow we felt that maybe we still had some family-- mine.
And he told them the way-- this is a story on its own, how we went to Poland and no papers and how we went back.
And we met up with my aunt, who survived, and his two sisters, who survived.
They eventually went to Canada, one who's still alive and one who died.
My aunt is not alive anymore.
She went-- eventually went to Israel, yeah.
And we went-- on the way, we met people and told us, don't go into your home towns.
The pogroms, the Poles are still killing Jews, those, the few that survived.
And I felt, now!
Me, I'm a big man now.
I'm not frightened.
This Monty Tabachnik went on to Lodz, because he was told his sisters were in Lodz.
I want to go back into town.
I got stopped at the station, went-- walked into the town.
It wasn't far.
And I was spat at, stoned at, abused at.
Look, there's still a Jew alive.
I managed to get as far as the square and no further.
There was the Polish and the Russian soldiers, standing, laughing.
They still found a Jew.
I turned around, went back to the station, went on to Lodz, the next train.
In Lodz, we met-- he met his sisters.
I met my aunt.
My aunt, obviously was delighted to see me.
I'll be her child from now on.
She will look after me.
I says, auntie, I'm not staying.
Where are you running to?
You've always been running.
She always told me, I was running.
I never stayed still.
I always tried to run.
Maybe that's why I stayed.
That's why I managed to-- to survive.
Always running, looking for hideouts, looking [INAUDIBLE].
I said, auntie, I'm not staying here.
I'm going back to where I came from, Theresienstadt.
I heard there was-- they were taking us either to Palestine or to England.
No, no England just Palestine. we were talking about taking the children.
And I, I left.
And she came in the station.
So did his two sisters, crying, begging us to stay behind.
No.
And we met another boy, that we met from the camp or we knew from the camp, [INAUDIBLE] [?
Meltz, ?] who lives in England now.
And all, all the papers we had was just little pieces of paper.
And we got to the-- the borders.
The Russians couldn't read.
I went with a piece of laundry paper through them.
And the other two walked behind me, underneath, and they went through.
And then I got through as well.
With a piece of laundry--
With a piece of laundry paper that we had in Theresienstadt or whatever it was.
But he tells me that we were arrested by the Poles on a certain station.
Wandering around, we didn't know what.
We didn't have no papers or nothing.
And they arrested us.
And I made such a noise, screaming and shouting, they let us go.
I didn't know that until he reminded me a couple of weeks ago about it.
Yes.
Well, by train from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Yes.
No, I don't remember the journey much.
No.
No No.
In Buchenwald, almost nothing.
Buchenwald, at that time, was divided into three different camps.
For your information, the chief rabbi, the present chief rabbi of Israel was in Buchenwald, Rabbi Lau.
Yes, I must have been.
I didn't know.
Some people remember him.
He was-- his is bit younger than us.
His older brother was with him.
He was-- managed to hide him out in different ways and I'll tell you that, in a minute, how it came about.
And in Buchenwald, we did nothing.
Buchenwald was something like a-- a transit camp, with taking people to work different places, different camp, different places.
We did nothing.
We just taking stones from one end and put them to another.
That was practically it.
We were given a certain ration of food.
It was a black bread, I remember.
One day, we got a little bit more.
I think it must have been with some margarine or something.
There were some people coming from the Red Cross.
So when you hear the people coming from the Red Cross, you always knew you were going to get some better rations.
The-- we never feared death.
We saw much of it in Buchenwald or even in other places.
Buchenwald, every morning, they were being carried out from the barracks and laid in piles.
We were in Block 66, as-- as I was told.
It was mostly young, young people, like myself.
I remember we-- I teamed up with a boy.
Again, I don't remember his name.
And we-- we decided the rations, that we were given, we should divide them up for twice in a day.
So we had one potion of ration, in the morning, another portion in the evening.
And I remember, one day, we had our morning ration, and we left.
We went out into the camp.
And we hid the second portion.
We hid, so we thought nobody would be able to find it.
And on the way back, we greeted by other people who said, oh, he stole your ration, or he stole the ration, the ration of food.
So we started running to see what it was ours, you know?
And by the time we found that it was our ration of food, and he was stuffing it in his mouth.
We ran after him.
Eventually, we got hold of him.
And he fell down.
I mean I remember taking stones and just throwing them.
But he didn't wait.
He just kept on chewing it, whatever it was, he kept on chewing.
We didn't turn around to see whether he was alive or dead.
We didn't.
No.
I don't know.
No.
There were quite a mixture of them.
I came across a guy who was brought back from the Russian front.
He said, somewhere, his generations back, there was some Jewishness in him.
So he was brought back from the Russian front into Buchenwald.
Buchenwald also had a lot of political prisoners, in fact, in the big camp.
And they were much better off than we were.
I was cleaning boots for somebody.
And he, now and again, he used to throw some food out of his window.
And he gave me, once, some food.
I don't know what it was, but little bones, and they were so sweet and soft that I left nothing behind there.
I don't know what it was.
Food, as far as I, personally, was concerned, was never such a big problem.
Always somehow managed to get some extra food.
Whether it was like cleaning boots or cleaning rooms for-- for other political prisoners or German officers, yeah, I always managed to-- to find.
I was fortunate in that respect.
Yeah.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
There must have been.
I mean almost bare feet.
I remember there was hardly anything on our feet even in winter.
We woken up, very often, in the mornings, and dark, cold, and taken into the wash-houses and washed in cold water.
There were many.
There were many.
I was fortunate.
That's all I can say.
I don't know whether it was the coin or whether it was because of my parents.
I didn't know.
I was fortunate to survive.
How, what?
I don't know.
I must have been at the right place at the right time.
That's all I can say.
Each one of us has different stories.
If you collate them, you must want to get the same story.
But different happenings, some worse than others.
If you hear some of my friend's sufferings, mine was very mild.
Well, only if I went to-- to clean his boots or his rooms, which what did we exchange?
Nothing.
I was only interested in what I was getting at the end of it, that's all.
Buchenwald, a bit more so.
We heard different things.
We heard planes come over and-- and dropping bombs nearby and so forth.
But that was towards the end of the war, already.
And we were talking about end of March-- end of March, I wasn't even in Buchenwald.
In Buchenwald?
There was no such thing.
I mean getting up early hours in the morning and stand on parade until everybody was counted.
And until they found out where the person who was missing, whether they was dead or whatever, you stood there in the cold.
And that's all.
And if you dropped, you dropped.
Very often, we saw-- then we get, at Buchenwald, it was already electrically wired.
And very often, you saw people running towards the wire, the barbed wire, and putting their hands on and getting electrocuted.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes, quite often.
Yes.
Just carried away.
It happened to me, once-- not in [INAUDIBLE], but in Buchenwald.
They used to bring in the big urns of soup, so-called soup to feed us.
It was mostly water.
And one day, we were standing, waiting, for this urn to come near us.
And there was a prisoner, from way back, two were carrying, all right, and a German, who was walking behind them, one-- mostly one, to shout before there were kapos.
And one prisoner was running across.
And he-- it was boiling water, however, it was hot.
He put his hand, right in, and grabbed the bottom, because you always-- the solid was at the bottom.
And he grabbed hold of that.
And as it-- it took seconds.
And the German took out a gun and killed him.
So he never enjoyed whatever he grabbed.
Couldn't have been much in it.
Not-- they were coming in, from time to time.
Wasn't in the camp.
You didn't see that many in the camp.
It was mostly controlled by kapos.
All kinds.
All kinds.
Yeah.
There were some Poles, as well.
Yeah.
Mostly Jews, yes.
Mm hm.
Only those people who tried to get through-- through the wires, whether they would think of escaping.
I don't think-- they knew, already, at that time, they couldn't escape.
Yeah, we-- we used to come across people that had escaped.
And old-- old or young, used to bring them back with a gun behind them and bring them back.
But--
Buchenwald, I was taken-- we were taken out of Buchenwald, it must have been the end of-- it took us four weeks, [INAUDIBLE] and we were liberated beginning of May.
So it must have been the end of March we were taken out of Buchenwald, suddenly.
We were told to march, march out of Buchenwald towards the station.
And-- and [INAUDIBLE] and those people who couldn't walk, were shot on the spot, quite a lot.
You never-- you never looked around.
You never looked back.
Only the ones who walk next to you, whether it was four, five abreast, or anything.
But if you heard the shot, you already knew that you couldn't walk anymore.
And sometimes, people carried, you know-- so they carried one another.
This wasn't-- wasn't this-- was this like a death march or was this being marched--
I don't know whether it started off as death march or not.
But we were taken to the trains, in open cattle tracks, loaded on, about 100 or so of us, you know?
And we were fortunate, mostly, on my-- on my train, it was mostly the youngsters from-- that we came over with in '45.
Mostly the '45 were in that transport.
Well, we didn't know where we were taken.
But it took us four weeks.
We ended up in Theresienstadt.
No, we didn't-- but on the train.
Traveling, yes.
Well, we didn't travel for four weeks, because they shunted, from line to line, making room for the escaping army.
They were trying-- we were told, we trying to take us to-- out to the camps.
But the camps were already liberated by the forces, the Allied forces, whether it was the Russians or-- or the Americans.
Mostly the Americans, because in Buchenwald, we came in-- back to Czechoslovakia.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes, I remember it very, very well.
One night, we were woken up.
Woken up?
We were-- opened up the wagons.
And we were given a certain type of food, which I can only describe-- poison.
It was a cabbage.
I remember that.
And we just gave a lick, and it was just burning us up.
And as youngsters, as I said to you, I wasn't-- we weren't that desperate for food.
We were desperate, but not-- we always managed to find something.
Because we were stopping on-- on the, on the journey quite often, mostly at nights, and making room.
You know, you could hear the trains being shunned from-- from line to line.
So we managed to always find-- and one time, we went through Sudetenland.
And they threw some stale bread into the wagon.
So we managed to find-- oh, or we managed to find some vegetables.
If the planes came down, attacking the trains, we ran into the fields to dig for food.
The Germans went under the wagons, the guards.
We ran into the fields to dig for-- so, and so we managed to find.
But this particular night, they woke up, then.
So we left it.
And we said, we'll wait until the next morning.
The next morning, all of us jumped out, those who were still alive, digging the ground, digging the earth for some moist to put in our mouth.
And we managed to find some extra water, eventually, and diluted it.
And we took bricks onto the wagons.
And we made little fires.
Or when we were stationed, a little fire, and we boiled it up, diluted the soup.
But many, many died that night.
You could see the lorries pulling up, by the, by the, by the railways, and carrying people.
Some weren't even dead yet.
Yeah.
Or you saw the time when the German officers or the German guards went into the-- into the railways and pulling teeth out of people who weren't dead.
Oh, yes.
Tch, gold, obviously.
They wouldn't take anything else.
Yeah.
We arrived in Theresienstadt almost days before the liberation, which was early May.
We were liberated, possibly, between the 5th and the 8th.
Some say-- people say 5th, some say the 8th, yeah, 1945.
By the Russians.
Yes.
How did it feel?
[LAUGHS] How did it feel?
At the beginning, all we wanted was to find some food.
No.
My-- my-- my ambition was, at the time, to be able to find a loaf of bread, to be able to cut slices.
That's what it was.
And when the Russians came in, the first couple of days, we could do what we wanted.
I made a pact with a guy, who was older than me, to go out, together.
We always tried to find other people to go together.
Never go by yourself.
Always go with somebody else.
And I said, whatever food you find, you give to me.
Whatever else I find, I'll give to you.
And we came-- we stopped groups of Germans, who were being evacuated, either into camps or-- or into centers, and we stopped them.
We took the food away from them.
One guy begged me to leave him his razor.
However much the Germans-- cleanliness, to them, is very-- and he begged me to leave him his razor.
I wasn't interested in that.
I was interested in finding food.
I remember coming into a house, an officer's house, and there was a big cupboard and a big picture of the officer.
And we looked through the house, but we couldn't find.
And this guy opened the cupboard, and he saw a lot of postage stamps.
To me, it didn't mean a thing.
But he knew what he was looking for.
And he took pages and pages of books of these postage stamps.
I was only interested in food.
And I came-- came back to the camp schlepping sacks of food for my friends.
Obviously.
Obviously.
Yeah.
To see whether we could find some family.
In fact, as I said before, within days, or it might have been weeks, we went back.
And this friend of mine and I went back to Poland looking for people.
It must have been end of May, June.
And we came back within weeks.
Yeah, Monty Tabachnik.
I went back to Ryki as I said before.
I didn't stay long.
I only stayed a few minutes, turned around and went back.
I met up with my aunt, who told us stories about pogroms.
She went and to her-- to her house.
She was introduced to the guy, a doctor, who lived in her house.
And he said, this is a woman, who's claiming her properties.
And the guard from the town-hall left.
And this guy, who occupied the house, said, if you don't go out now, they'll carry you out at night.
So you better be aware.
And she said, that night, other people, Jews, who were still left from the camps, survived, there was a pogrom, quite a few were killed.
She managed to hide yourself in a haystack and escape.
My aunt, eventually, was in Lodz, yes.
Yes.
Lodz, I don't remember.
My-- I was only there a few days.
Yeah.

You see there was-- I mean, the movements of the people then, there was always running to-- what we call it-- like the town-halls or the Jewish judenrat, to try and find names or whatever or getting vouchers for food.
Nobody had anything.
So it was only controlled by the Jewish community.
And they, in turn, I suppose, by then, were getting it either from England and from-- from-- from America, from the [?
Joint. ?] And that's how you, you-- the contact you had.
Wherever you went, there was thousands of people.
Thousands?
There were hundreds of people for the same reason, either getting vouchers for food or getting food or getting some sort of start now.
That's all it was.
And then, from Lodz, you went back to Theresienstadt.
Yes.
Well, we-- we-- we were told that we were going to go on to Palestine, the youth.
Yeah.
And I had nothing left in Poland.
And I didn't want to be a-- a baby anymore.
My aunt wanted to bring me up as a-- as her child.
She lost all her family.
Ah, I suppose, on hindsight, yes.
Yes, I suppose so.
But on the other hand, who knows what my destiny would have been otherwise.
I can't tell.
Obviously, my destiny was to come here and bring up a family.
We went.
We were taken.
The whole group were taken to Prague.
Well, the first group had 300 of us.
We came to England.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Some came in after.
They weren't all liberated at the same time.
Some came from other camps.
We weren't all in Buchenwald.
Some came in from other camps as well.
Monty Tabachnik came from-- from others, other places, yeah.
We were separated.
Yes.
300 of us came-- came, yes.
The first 300 came in August, 1945, from Theresienstadt via Prague.
And we were-- now it's well-known, that the boys, the book, written by Sir Martin Gilbert, about the boys.
And the British government, in those days, gave permission for 1,000 children to be brought over.
But they couldn't find 1,000 children.
They could only find 732.
And we were the first 300 that came over.
1945, August the 14th.
Well, I tell you something, I seem to remember that, in Theresienstadt, coming back from, from Poland, I was asked to make a speech in English.
I was-- they had it written out in phonetics.
And that that's-- I read out.
What I read, I don't know.
Who I read it to, I don't know.
At the same time, it would be given in Windemere, in England, already.
I presented the deputation with an album of all us.
Didn't mean-- the language didn't-- whether it was English or whatever.
I didn't speak English.
I read something out, out of this paper, that's all.
Why me, I was picked, I don't know.
And eventually, we mostly spoke Yiddish amongst-- amongst ourselves and Polish.
Polish was my first language.
I must have hated it all my life, Polish.
Yiddish was our language at home.
